quote: Is there anything else that interested you in solar or just getting an estimate to see?
stewart_read: Carter probed for emotional depth on the interest question, which is the right instinct per the schema. But when Justin answered 'Just trying to get an estimate to be honest,' Carter accepted the surface answer and moved on. The schema flags this item as the emotional anchor of the call — 'depth matters more than the answer' and 'top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Carter spent roughly six seconds. The reactive read is that Carter missed the depth probe. The strategic read — that Carter was preserving rapport with a transactional customer who would resist a longer probe — is plausible but unsupported; there is no evidence Carter attempted a second angle before moving on.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter read Justin as a transactional customer who would disengage under a longer emotional probe, and deliberately kept the interest question light to preserve momentum.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the schema note on verify.interest_in_solar. The question was asked — the follow-through wasn't there. In the 1-on-1, ask Carter: 'What would you have done if Justin had said something beyond the estimate?' The goal is to build one more angle for when the customer gives a shallow first answer — not to interrogate, but to open a crack. Illustrative (not Ion-validated): 'What's driving the timing — is the bill getting to you, or is it more about the house being new and wanting to set it up right?'

stewart_read: Carter received the bill anchor — $180/month on a brand-new home — and responded with 'Okay. Perfect.' before moving to the next verify item. The schema is explicit: capture alone is not sufficient. The bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. Carter had the number and the moment; neither was used. This is the universal floor coaching gap the schema identifies across the corpus.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter deferred the flip to avoid slowing a cooperative customer who was already moving toward the appointment. Not supported — there is no later moment in the call where Carter returned to the bill as leverage.
coaching_implication: This is the primary coaching beat for this call (see coaching_focus priority 1). Play the 02:15 moment in the 1-on-1 and ask Carter what he could have done with that number before moving on. The flip doesn't require fabricated savings figures — the pain the number represents is the leverage. Illustrative (not Ion-validated): 'A hundred and eighty a month — that's over two grand a year going to Dominion. That's exactly what we're trying to fix.' The manager should confirm Ion-validated phrasing before deploying.

quote: I am active duty. Yes. Navy. Four years. Roughly going on five years.
stewart_read: Justin's military disclosure is an enthusiasm signal on two dimensions: (1) it surfaces a discount eligibility Carter acknowledged ('we'll be sure to take advantage of that'), and (2) active-duty Navy with a brand-new home is a long-tenure signal — this customer is likely to be at this address for years, which strengthens the solar ROI case and the appointment's value. Carter thanked Justin for his service and noted the discount, which is the right human move. What Carter did not do is use the tenure signal to deepen Justin's investment in the appointment — the 'you'll be here long enough to see the full return' angle was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter's handling was adequate for the setter scope — the discount acknowledgment is a legitimate rapport move and the tenure angle is closer-tier content.
coaching_implication: Not a failure — Carter handled the moment warmly. Surface this in the 1-on-1 as a 'what else was available here' conversation, not a correction. The tenure angle ('you just moved in, you'll be here for years — this is the right time to lock in') is a curiosity-creation move the setter can use without crossing into closer-tier content.

quote: Okay. Awesome. And that's when I'll get the, rough estimate and all that. Correct?
stewart_read: Justin's confirmation question reveals that his mental model of the appointment is 'I'm getting a rough estimate' — not 'I'm getting a custom solar design for my specific home.' Carter confirmed with 'Yeah.' This is a multi-touch pattern endpoint (see multi_touch_patterns): Justin entered with this frame at 00:11 and exits with it intact. The closer will walk into an appointment where the customer expects a ballpark, not a proposal process. This is a show-rate and closer-handoff risk.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter's 'Yeah' is technically accurate — the closer will show numbers. The reframe risk is overstated if the closer opens by anchoring to the custom design rather than a generic estimate.
coaching_implication: Flag for the handoff brief (see below). In the 1-on-1, ask Carter: 'When Justin said rough estimate, what did you hear?' The goal is to build the habit of reframing 'estimate' as 'your specific design' before the appointment — not to oversell, but to set the closer up correctly. Illustrative (not Ion-validated): 'Yeah — it won't be a rough estimate, it'll be a full design built off your actual usage. That's why we need the bill.'

quote: can you see anything getting in the way of that appointment, or should that be good?
stewart_read: Carter executed a variant of the button_up handshake — not the canonical 'Is that fair?' but a functionally equivalent commitment question. Justin confirmed ('No. That should be good.'). This is a positive execution moment. The schema marks this element as predictive of show rate and flags it as a skip risk. Carter did not skip it.
coaching_implication: Positive reinforcement moment for the 1-on-1. Carter's phrasing is a natural variant of the canonical handshake — strategy-aligned per the schema's strategy_recognition directive. Name it explicitly so Carter knows this is a habit worth keeping.

quote: I'll send you a confirmation once I have that in the schedule for you.
stewart_read: Carter promised a confirmation text but did not commit to a tieback — the schema requires the text to reference something specific the customer said on the call. The confirmation as described ('I have that in the schedule for you') is generic. The tieback material was available: Justin's military service, the brand-new home, the Dominion bill. None of these were named as the anchor for the follow-up text.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have sent a personalized text after the call ended — the transcript does not capture post-call activity. The gap is in the on-call promise, not necessarily the text itself.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Carter what he put in the confirmation text. If it was generic, walk through the tieback requirement. The military angle is the obvious hook — it's personal, it's memorable, and it differentiates the text from a calendar invite. Illustrative (not Ion-validated): 'Hey Justin, Carter with ION — locked you in for Friday at 6PM. Looking forward to showing you what we can put together for the new house. Thank you for your service.'

quote: they'll be coming by. We've locked you out for an hour just so you don't feel rushed
stewart_read: Carter stated the one-hour block (button_up.one_hour_block_stated — compliant) but did not deliver the 'not driving, at your computer' anchor required by button_up.no_driving_at_computer. The schema notes that skipping this element raises no-show rate. Additionally, 'they'll be coming by' implies an in-person visit rather than a phone appointment — button_up.phone_appointment_specified requires this to be explicit. On a Friday 6PM appointment with an active-duty Navy customer, the channel ambiguity is a real no-show risk.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have clarified the phone format earlier in the scheduling exchange (05:26–06:12) in a way the transcript does not fully capture. The 'they'll be coming by' phrasing is the only channel signal in the transcript.
coaching_implication: Two gaps in one moment: (1) phone vs. in-person was not explicitly confirmed, and (2) the computer/not-driving anchor was skipped. In the 1-on-1, walk Carter through the full button_up checklist and ask him to self-identify what was missing. The phone-appointment specification is the higher-risk gap given the 'coming by' language.

quote: the only requirements are that you pay income tax and have a credit score above six fifty. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Carter stated the credit threshold as 650. The schema schema records 670 as the threshold, and flags a prior instance (SESSION18_fcbf33c1) where the same 650 drift was observed. This is a schema drift pattern — either the active policy is 650 and the schema needs updating, or Carter is consistently understating the threshold. On this call the outcome is unaffected (Justin qualified), but the drift is a floor-level data point worth surfacing to Kenny.
alternative_hypothesis: The active Ion policy may be 650, not 670 — the schema itself flags this as unresolved pending Kenny's confirmation.
coaching_implication: Do not coach Carter on this in isolation — it requires Kenny to resolve the threshold question first (see qualifiers.credit_qualifier policy_question_for_kenny). Flag the drift to Kenny as a second data point alongside SESSION18_fcbf33c1. Once the correct threshold is confirmed, update the floor script and brief all setters.
